summary: I was born in Yamanashi Prefecture on February 15, 1888, during the twenty-first year of Meiji. My family had been farming there for generations, and we owned about seven or eight cho of land, roughly two and a half acres. Growing up in Yamanashi, I was surrounded by a rural farming community, and it was a place where I attended elementary and middle school before leaving Japan.

I was born in the twenty-first year of Meiji, which corresponds to 1888. The Meiji era was a time of great change in Japan, and my schooling reflected the educational reforms of that period. It was during Meiji that I attended elementary and middle school, learning subjects like Japanese, arithmetic, and history.

I had a strong interest in studying beyond the regular school curriculum, so I attended night school where I learned Chinese history and Chinese classics. A famous scholar of Chinese classics in my village taught me these subjects, and I really enjoyed studying them. This extra learning helped me feel more accomplished during my school days.

Mr. Higuchi was my elementary school teacher and also a priest. He was a very serious person and left a strong impression on me. I remember him well among the teachers I had during my schooling in Japan.

Mr. Kaneko was the principal of my school before Mr. Shinohara took over. I remember him as part of my school days, though it was Mr. Shinohara who later showed a particular interest in me. Kaneko's leadership was part of the school environment I experienced.

Mr. Shinohara became our principal after Mr. Kaneko left, and he took a special interest in me as a student. Unlike the usual custom, he awarded me an honor prize upon graduation because he thought I was extraordinary and would become useful in the future. This recognition meant a lot to me, and even years later my family kept the certificate as a proud reminder.

The Russo-Japanese War started when I was seventeen, and it influenced my decision to leave Japan. I did not want to be drafted into the military, so I gave up my plan to attend normal school and instead prepared to go to the United States. Japan's victory in the war, including the fall of Port Arthur in 1905, also affected how Japanese immigrants were perceived abroad.

My family was Buddhist, but when my mother was sick, a Tenrikyo priest came to pray for her. The priest advised us to change doctors, and after following his suggestion, my mother recovered quickly. Since then, my mother believed in Tenrikyo, though the rest of the family did not take much interest in it.

I only heard about Christianity once in Japan from a high school student in my village who had been influenced by it. He spoke about Christianity during summer vacation, and I went to listen to him. Otherwise, Christianity was not something my family or I had much contact with.

I came to the United States in 1905 when I was eighteen, leaving Japan to avoid military conscription during the Russo-Japanese War. I first landed in San Francisco and was surprised by the hostility toward Japanese immigrants at that time. Despite the rough welcome, I was hopeful about opportunities here and worked briefly at the Nichibei Times newspaper.

On my way to the United States mainland, I stopped over in Hawaii. There I saw many young men from various parts of Japan, including Kyushu, Hiroshima, and Tohoku, all speaking their own dialects. It was mostly men traveling alone, as many had not yet brought their wives from Japan, and the picture bride system had not yet become common.

Many of the young men I met on the ship to Hawaii and later in the United States came from Kyushu. They spoke the Kyushu dialect, which I did not understand well since I was from Yamanashi. These men were part of the early wave of Japanese immigrants working hard in America.

There were also quite a few immigrants from Hiroshima among the group traveling to Hawaii and the United States. They spoke the Hiroshima dialect, and like those from Kyushu and Tohoku, they formed distinct groups. I was one of the few from Yamanashi among them.

Some of the immigrants I encountered came from the Tohoku region. They spoke their own dialect and traveled in groups with others from their area. This regional grouping was common among Japanese immigrants at the time.

San Francisco was my first destination in the United States in 1905. Unfortunately, my first impression was quite negative because of the strong anti-Japanese sentiment there. I was met with insults and even had horse dung thrown at me upon arrival. Despite this, I found some support through the Nichibei Times, where I worked setting type for a few months.

Port Arthur was a heavily fortified Russian port that Japan captured during the Russo-Japanese War in 1905. This victory was significant and caused fear among Americans, especially government officials and the press. The fall of Port Arthur symbolized Japan's military strength and influenced how Japanese immigrants were viewed in the United States.

The Chronicles was a Los Angeles newspaper that wrote negatively about Japanese immigrants around the time of the Russo-Japanese War. It published articles urging the United States to keep Japanese people out, reflecting the early anti-Japanese movement. This media hostility contributed to the difficult reception Japanese newcomers like me faced.

After arriving in San Francisco, I was helped by some young men from Yamanashi who worked at the Nichibei Times newspaper. They offered me a job setting type, which I did for about three months. The Nichibei Times was a place where Japanese immigrants could find some support despite the widespread hostility in the city.
